https://www.straitstimes.com/opinion/st-editorial/dribs-and-drabs-of-fake-news-can-turn-entire-society-against-authority

Dribs and drabs of fake news can turn entire society against authority
Tan Ooi Boon
Associate Foreign Editor
JAN 4, 2020, 5:00 AM SGT

Recent use of the anti-fake news law is necessary to nip public misinformation in the bud, as the case of the Hong Kong police force shows
........
Just look no further than Hong Kong to see how such a sustained attack could leave the trust in its police force - once lauded as one of the best in Asia - in tatters.

A man lighting joss sticks outside Prince Edward MTR station at a protest in Hong Kong last November. One example of fake news is the murder of three protesters during a police crackdown at the station on Aug 31. Makeshift funeral altars were put up outside the station though the police said no one died.

A recent opinion poll there showed that the force's public satisfaction rating fell to its lowest at 35 points now, down from 61 points in the beginning of June last year before the anti-government protest erupted.

While the protesters there might argue that the force deserved the low rating for police brutality inflicted on their compatriots, the fact is the cops have also lost much public trust due to many fake allegations that were hurled at them with impunity.

Some of the more serious fake news includes:

• Accusations that Hong Kong police officers are gang rapists and that they have attacked numerous women during the protests. These allegations were put online via podcasts, videos and reports. In a recent one, the speaker in a podcast claimed that a group of officers at the detention centre took turns to rape a woman detainee. "They are worse than the Japanese soldiers during the war." The lawyer for an alleged victim had filed a report with the police complaint unit. Even so, the police have refuted this allegation as their initial probe showed that the girl was not even present at the station where the incident allegedly happened.

• The murder of three protesters during a police crackdown at Prince Edward MTR station on Aug 31. Various people who claimed to be friends or relatives of the alleged victims went online to "confirm" that their kin had indeed vanished, because they were murdered and their bodies were disposed of by the cops. Shamans and fengshui masters also jumped in because they claimed to be able to see the souls of the alleged victims and asserted that the entities were still trapped inside the station because they were murdered there. Such reports resulted in makeshift funeral altars being put up twice outside the station even though the police repeatedly said no one died in the incident.

• The police caused the death of 15-year-old Youth College student Chan Yin Lam who was found drowned in the sea last September. Apart from various fake news reports, another shaman was used to "confirm" that her death was caused by "people with power". The police said they had never come into contact with the girl since the protest started in June last year. This was supported by her mother who went on a television interview to say that her daughter had probably committed suicide as she was suffering from a psychosis prior to her death.

• The police caused the death of 22-year-old university student Chow Tsz Lok who died in an apparent fall in a carpark last November. While no one saw how the incident happened, various reports fingered the police for either firing tear gas pellets into the carpark, which indirectly led to his death, or literally chasing and throwing him down the carpark. The police said all these were untrue, and this was supported by closed-circuit television footage that showed neither signs of tear smoke nor police presence inside the carpark around the time of the fall. They believed the victim fell one storey to his death because he could have mistakenly leapt over a ledge, thinking that there was an adjacent landing. News reports also noted that help for the victim was delayed because the ambulance crew had to navigate through several illegal road blocks set up by protesters.

Such a constant barrage of allegations has given rise to genuine fear among many residents there, who now seem to be convinced that the law enforcers, once considered among the best in Asia, have somehow become a bunch of thugs and rapists almost overnight.

Indeed, a woman protester who spoke to The Straits Times recently actually made this startling comment: "If I get arrested, I would rather commit suicide than to allow (the police) to rape me."
...................
They should learn from some civic-minded residents in Hong Kong who stepped forward to become online vigilantes to strike back at fake news.
